definition: Compressing what a large teacher model knows into a smaller student by training the student to imitate the teacher's outputs rather than learn only from hard labels. The premise is that the teacher's full probability distribution carries far more than a single correct label, its "dark knowledge" of which wrong answers are nearly right, and in the LLM era distillation often means simply training a student on a strong model's generated outputs.

body.text: Knowledge distillation transfers what a large, capable model knows into a smaller, cheaper one. The large model is the teacher and the small model the student, and instead of training the student only on the original dataset's ground-truth labels, distillation trains it to match the teacher's outputs. The premise is that the teacher's full output, especially its probability distribution over all possible answers, carries far more information than a single correct label, so a small model can learn faster and better by absorbing that richer signal.

Distillation matters because deployment economics often demand small models while quality demands large ones, and it is one of the main ways to get both. A model with billions of parameters may be too slow or expensive to serve at scale or to run on a phone, yet training a small model from scratch yields weaker results, so distillation lets a compact student inherit much of a large teacher's competence, narrowing the quality gap that pure size would otherwise impose. It is a workhorse of model compression alongside quantization and pruning.

The classic mechanism trains the student on the teacher's soft targets: the full probability distribution the teacher assigns across the output vocabulary, usually softened with a temperature so small probabilities are not crushed to zero. These soft targets encode the teacher's relative confidence and the similarity structure it perceives among options, which wrong answers are nearly right, often called dark knowledge. The student minimizes a loss that pulls its distribution toward the teacher's, sometimes blended with the ordinary loss on the true labels, and variants also match intermediate hidden representations or attention patterns, not just final outputs.

In the era of large language models, distillation has taken on a looser, very common form: simply generating a large set of high-quality outputs from a strong teacher model and using them as supervised fine-tuning or instruction-tuning data for a smaller student. Many capable open models were trained substantially on data distilled this way, which makes distillation a primary engine of the proliferation of small, strong models, though it raises questions about licensing and about errors propagating from teacher to student.

Knowledge distillation is a specific and powerful instance of transfer learning, moving knowledge between models rather than between tasks. It complements pretraining and fine-tuning by addressing the size axis: where those stages decide what a model knows, distillation decides how small the model carrying that knowledge can be. It also pairs naturally with architectures like mixture-of-experts, where a large sparse teacher can supervise a smaller dense student for efficient serving.

image.alt: A flow diagram. An input x feeds both a teacher model and a smaller student model. The teacher's logits pass through a softmax with temperature to give soft labels. The student produces soft predictions at the same temperature and a hard prediction at temperature one; a distillation loss compares the student's soft predictions to the teacher's soft labels, and a student loss compares the hard prediction to the ground-truth label.
image.caption: Knowledge distillation trains a small student model to imitate a large teacher rather than learning only from hard labels. The teacher's full probability distribution, softened with a temperature, becomes a soft target that carries which wrong answers are nearly right. The student minimizes a distillation loss against those soft labels, often blended with the ordinary loss on the true label, inheriting much of the teacher's competence in a smaller network.
